data.title: VDY: There Is A Better Canadian Dividend Option In XDIV
data.description:
All figures are in $CAD unless otherwise stated.

All financial information comes from Seeking Alpha unless otherwise stated.

VDY Not The Best In The Strategy

Over the past 5 years, the Vanguard FTSE Canadian High Dividend Yield Index ETF (TSX:VDY:CA) has outperformed all of the larger ETFs with a similar mandate. Over that same period, VDY has also outperformed the SPTSX.

As many of us know, past performance does not guarantee future returns and we believe that to be the case for VDY. While we expect that VDY will track the SPTSX quite closely going forward, we believe there are better options with a similar mandate (i.e. High Yield Canadian Dividend Stocks) due to VDY's poor overall diversification and ETFs with a lower MER. More specifically, we believe XDIV:CA is the better option.

Portfolio Diversification is Misleading

The largest ten holdings in VDY make up 72% of the ETFs holdings as of December 31, 2024. In contrast, XDIVs top ten holdings made up 80% of the weight. At first glance, it actually appears as if VDY is more diversified than XDIV and this metric shows up again with the number of holdings. VDY has 56 holdings and XDIV only has 20. VDY's lack of diversification becomes more prominent when you look at its largest holding, historical risk metrics, and sector exposures.

While VDY has 56 holdings, the largest holding is 16.03% of the ETF (RY:CA) whereas XDIV's largest holding with only 20 holdings is 9.69% (TD:CA). With 56 holdings in VDY and 72% being in the top ten, there is only 28% of weight left to distribute across 46 holdings. In fact, VDY's two largest holdings (2nd largest holding is TD:CA) make up 25% of the weight which means the largest two holdings are almost equal in contribution to the price return as the smallest 46 holdings. Ironically, the other pitfall we would expect from this strategy is higher trading costs and turnover as there are more holdings however, we believe it's important to note that XDIV actually has a higher turnover ratio and trading expense.

Standard deviation (or volatility) measures how much an investment's returns fluctuate. That is, a higher standard deviation means more ups and more downs. The Sharpe Ratio tells you whether or not an investment's returns are worth the risk as it compares the return to volatility. That is, a higher Sharpe ratio means you're getting more return for each unit of risk or a better 'risk-adjusted' performance. The upside capture ratio measures how much of the market's gains an investment captures and the downside capture ratio measures how much of the market's losses an investment captures. That is, you want an upside capture ratio of greater than 100 and a downside capture ratio of less than 100. For example, if the downside capture ratio for VDY is 80, when the market falls 10%, VDY would theoretically only fall 8%. Over the past 3 years, the table below summarizes these key performance/risk metrics for VDY and XDIV:

ETF Volatility Sharpe Ratio Upside Capture Downside Capture
VDY 13.58% 0.36 95 108
XDIV 11.56% 0.62 86 77

What we quickly notice is that XDIV has offered much better risk-adjusted performance and when the market is 'down', XDIV does a much better job insulating losses. However, in an 'up' market, VDY does a better job capitalizing on gains. We highlight the importance of these metrics because we believe that many investors who utilize these ETFs as a part of their portfolio are retirees looking for dividends to supplement their lifestyle. In order to preserve/grow your wealth, you should want something that is going to have reasonable capital growth, high dividend yield, and relatively low volatility. We believe that XDIV is the better option here as VDY and XDIV are essentially interchangeable from a capital growth perspective, XDIV has a higher forward dividend yield of 4.24% versus VDY at 3.97% (growth rates in dividends are also very similar - data is from Seeking Alpha), and XDIV has historically offered lower risk as seen above.

Finally, we turn our attention to the sector exposures (data from each respective ETF provider as at December 31, 2024) which can be seen in the table below:

Sector VDY XDIV Difference
Financials 58.54% 45.80% 12.74%
Energy 29.64% 33.27% -3.63%
Utilities 5.08% 11.66% -6.58%
Telecommunications 4.37% 3.98% 0.39%
Basic Materials 1.01% 0.00% 1.01%
Consumer Cyclical 0.62% 5.29% -4.67%
Real Estate 0.27% 0.00% 0.27%
Industrials 0.26% 0.00% 0.26%
Consumer Defensive 0.17% 0.00% 0.17%
Technology 0.05% 0.00% 0.05%
Healthcare 0.00% 0.00% 0.00%
Other 0.00% 0.00% 0.00%

As seen in the table above, VDY has a much larger exposure to the Financials sector followed by slightly larger exposures in Basic Materials and Telecommunications. Where VDY is underweight compared to XDIV is Energy, Utilities, and Consumer Cyclical Stocks. Our attention is mainly geared at the near 60% exposure of VDY to the financials sector. If the Financial sector were to experience a recession (e.g. high levels of default, slower capital markets), VDY would be heavily exposed. We note that XDIV isn't much better from this point of view however, having higher exposure to other sectors that aren't as correlated with the market (i.e. Energy) or sectors that are viewed as less risky (i.e. Utilities), somewhat makes up for this pitfall.

With all of the information presented above, we believe XDIV is less risky for investors and offers retirees who look for this mandate a better path to preserving their wealth.

So Who's Management Expense Ratio is Better?

VDY currently has an MER of 0.22% and XDIV currently has an MER of 0.11%. Assuming an investment in each of $10,000.00, a 5% annual return, and a 10-year holding period, you could expect to pay VDY $280 in management fees whereas with the same assumptions, you could expect to pay $141 in management fees. This may not seem like much at first but with a sizeable investment, these differences add up and when we consider the points we mentioned above, we would prefer to pay for the lower fee ETF. Not to mention, both of these ETFs essentially replicate the performance of the overall Canadian stock market so a lower MER in our opinion is worth it.

Finally, while VDY is the larger fund with $3.4 billion in total assets (XDIV has $2 billion), RBC iShares has more AUM than Vanguard and by investing XDIV, you are supporting a Canadian corporation versus an American corporation (not that this matters, we thought it was something fun to point out).

Conclusion

In summary, we believe that XDIV is the better option for retirees looking at VDY for their next investment as it appears to be the safer choice and investors can sleep well at night knowing they are getting one of the lowest MERs in the country for this mandate. This article is not an opposition against VDY as we believe the ETF can fit nicely into the right person's portfolio. We feel that XDIV is just the better option for a larger set of investors.

data.title: Nvidia: A Buy For Bold Investors Despite DeepSeek's Cost Claims
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation's (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:NVDA) stock has stalled over the last several months due to valuation concerns, making the stock sensitive to the market selling off the stock if the company's high revenue growth and profitability come under an actual or perceived threat. An example of a perceived threat to revenue growth was when DeepSeek's (DEEPSEEK) news of training an AI model at lower costs than OpenAI's model hit the market, opening up the possibility that demand could fall for Nvidia's powerful AI chip. The market's immediate reaction was to sell off the stock. It dropped around 17% on January 27. While it has recovered some since then, the DeepSeek news continues to weigh down the stock.

Before the DeepSeek news hit the market, I upgraded NVDA stock to a Buy rating for only aggressive growth investors on December 10, 2024. Although the stock's high valuation and risks are genuine, I reasoned that the market may undervalue the company's estimated +50% revenue and earnings growth rate in its fiscal year (“FY”) 2026. However, this buy rating is only appropriate for investors willing to accept the stock's significant potential downside if the company misses quarterly results expectations or the long-term technological threat of customers reducing their reliance on Nvidia's increasingly powerful AI chips becomes more immediate.

This article will discuss the DeepSeek threat and why it's not a concern for Nvidia over the near and medium term. It will examine its third-quarter earnings call and what to expect when the company reports fourth-quarter results. The article will also review Nvidia's long-term risks and valuation, and why it is a buy for aggressive growth investors willing to speculate on the AI infrastructure market.

The DeepSeek Threat

It is difficult to question Nvidia's short- and medium-term dominance because the AI chip market is supply constrained. However, once DeepSeek came onto the scene, claiming that it could train AI models at substantially lower costs, it exposed a latent fear among some Nvidia bulls that technological advances in AI models could make increasing the raw computing power in chips less necessary over the long term. Customers could shift to chips that are powerful enough but consume less energy, which may neuter some of Nvidia's head start in creating increasingly more powerful AI chips.

Before I go further into this discussion, it's essential to understand that the conversation around DeepSeek can be confusing for a non-technical person. I had to revise this discussion about DeepSeek several times because I discovered that some articles about the topic have some misconceptions. The first thing to understand is that there are three steps in training an AI model:

  • Pretraining (before the model use)
  • Post-training (after the models use)
  • Test-time scaling (during the inferencing use of the model).

I will explain what pretraining an AI model entails later in this article, but it is likely the most expensive step. Post-training is like fine-tuning the AI model for specific topics or use cases, and is less costly because training datasets are smaller and training takes less time than pretraining. Test-time scaling takes place during inferencing, the time the AI model is generating a response to a prompt. Test-time scaling is the lowest cost out of all three training steps.

A few initial articles discussing how DeepSeek lowered costs focused on pretraining costs. For instance, Bain and Company recently published an article that stated:

What sets DeepSeek apart is the prospect of radical cost efficiency. The company claims to have trained its model for just $6 million using 2,000 Nvidia H800 graphics processing units ("GPUs") vs. the $80 million to $100 million cost of GPT-4 and the 16,000 H100 GPUs required for Meta's LLaMA 3​. While the comparisons are far from apples to apples, the possibilities are valuable to understand.

This statement alarmed some people because if DeepSeek's claims are valid, and it is possible to create models the equivalent of GPT-4 or LLaMa 3 at far less cost with much fewer GPUs, that could, in the long term, create far less demand for NVIDIA's highest-end GPUs. It also raises the possibility that companies could train and inference their AI models on more price-performant (good enough performance at a lower cost) AI chips, such as Amazon's (AMZN) Trainum chips. Amazon's Chief Executive Officer Andy Jassy said on the company's recent fourth quarter 2024 earnings call:

Trainium 2 just launched at our AWS Reinvent Conference in December and EC2 instances with these chips are typically 30% to 40% more price performant than other current GPU-powered instances available. That's very compelling at scale. Several technically capable companies like Adobe (ADBE), Databricks, poolside, and Qualcomm (QCOM) have seen impressive results in early testing of Trainium 2. It's also why you're seeing Anthropic build their future frontier models on Trainium 2. We're collaborating with Anthropic to build Project Rainier, a cluster of Trainium 2 ultra servers containing hundreds of thousands of Trainium 2 chips. This cluster is going to be 5 times the number of exaflops as the cluster that Anthropic used to train their current leading set of cloud models.

Nvidia will likely not see any adverse impact from competition in a supply constrained AI chip market. However, over the long term, as supply and demand balance out, companies like Marvell (MRVL), Broadcom (AVGO), and others building increasingly more price-performant chips may make it more challenging for Nvidia to grow at the rates investors require to justify its valuation — or at least that is one bearish theory.

Don't underestimate Nvidia

The need for Nvidia to create increasingly more powerful chips may not diminish for quite a while, if ever. While DeepSeek may have developed an innovative AI model, the company's cost-efficiency claims for pretraining an AI model may be far less than those cited in the Bain news article. Semianalysis published a news article on January 31, 2025, that stated:

DeepSeek's price and efficiencies caused the frenzy this week, with the main headline being the "$6M" dollar figure training cost of DeepSeek V3. This is wrong. This akin to pointing to a specific part of a bill of materials for a product and attributing it as the entire cost. The pretraining cost is a very narrow portion of the total cost...The $6M cost in the paper is attributed to just the GPU cost of the pretraining run, which is only a portion of the total cost of the model. Excluded are important pieces of the puzzle like R&D and TCO of the hardware itself. For reference, Claude 3.5 Sonnet cost $10s of millions to train, and if that was the total cost Anthropic needed, then they would not raise billions from Google and tens of billions from Amazon. It's because they have to experiment, come up with new architectures, gather and clean data, pay employees, and much more.

Pretraining a large language model (“LLM”) involves feeding the model massive datasets consisting of publicly accessible text online, such as books, articles, web pages, code, and video transcripts, to learn language patterns. The costs for pretraining consist of money spent on hardware (GPUs, memory, and networking equipment), data acquisition and cleaning, AI and Data engineer employee expenses, Research and Development (R&D), and operational costs such as server maintenance and utility costs. What the above statement implies is that DeepSeek only included the costs of buying or renting the GPU and possibly the utility costs of using the GPU. It excluded R&D expenses, data acquisition and cleaning costs, labor, and ongoing expenses related to server maintenance, API usage, and infrastructure scaling (adding more computing, storage, and networking resources). In short, the above commentary means the overall costs for training an AI model involve more than just GPU costs, and the $6 million figure may be deceptive.

OpenAI released a full version of its latest AI model, GPT-o1, on December 05, 2024. DeepSeek released its latest R-1 model on January 20, 2025. I haven't found a communication from OpenAI outlining how much it costs to develop GPT-o1, so it is difficult to compare developmental costs between DeepSeek R-1 and OpenAI GPT-o1. However, I wouldn't be surprised if the total cost to create the DeepSeek-R1 model and GpT-o1 are similar.

On the day the DeepSeek news roiled the markets, Seeking Alpha published an article that stated,

“'DeepSeek is an excellent AI advancement and a perfect example of Test Time Scaling,' an Nvidia spokesperson told Seeking Alpha in response to the stock market selloff.”

Test Time Scaling is a new AI paradigm that adds extra computing power at the point the AI model generates text and images or makes a prediction. This additional computing power gives the AI more time to “think” or reason, theoretically leading to better answers. More “thinking time” may require more powerful AI chips, meaning that Test Time Scaling could lead to higher demand for Nvidia's chips over price-performant chips. Theoretically, the AI may take longer to respond if price-performant chips cannot provide the same inferencing power as NVIDIA's chips. Depending on an organization's need for its AI to respond quickly, it still may want to use Nvidia's chips over Amazon's Trainium chips, for instance.

Going deeper down the rabbit hole, both R-1 and GPT-o1 use Time Test Scaling when inferencing. The comparison between the two AI models when responding to questions may be the primary reason people are interested in DeepSeek, not the pretraining costs. Seeking Alpha discourages linking to specific YouTube videos, so I will refrain from doing so here. However, if you want to see a few comparisons of the two AI models answering questions, Google “GPT-01 versus DeepSeek comparison,” and several videos will pop up.

One big difference between the two AI models is that DeepSeek's models are free and open source. In contrast, GPT-o1 is only available to premium users of ChatGPT for $20 a month for limited service and $200 for unlimited service through ChatGPT pro plan. Be aware that the o1 model through the ChatGPT pro plan is slightly more advanced than the AI model on the $20 limited service. This free versus open-source battle illustrates how open-source models could potentially hurt companies using an AI-as-a-service business model.

In response to questions, R-1 appears to have a more extensive reasoning process for generating an answer. The $20/month plan GPTo1 service typically has a shorter reasoning process behind obtaining a final answer than R-1. In the videos that I have watched, GPT-o1 ($20/month version) responds (“thinks”) faster, but occasionally gets difficult questions wrong. The $200 version of GPT-o1 answered the questions correctly that the $20 of GPT-o1 got wrong. The free R-1 and the $200 GPT-o1 appear to be equivalent services. However, there are reasons enterprises would choose GPT-o1 over R-1. KrebsonSecurity recently published an article that stated:

New mobile apps from the Chinese artificial intelligence ((AI)) company DeepSeek have remained among the top three "free" downloads for Apple and Google devices since their debut on January 25, 2025. But experts caution that many of DeepSeek's design choices — such as using hard-coded encryption keys, and sending unencrypted user and device data to Chinese companies — introduce a number of glaring security and privacy risks... DeepSeek's rapid rise caught the attention of the mobile security firm NowSecure, a Chicago-based company that helps clients screen mobile apps for security and privacy threats. In a teardown of the DeepSeek app published today, NowSecure urged organizations to remove the DeepSeek iOS mobile app from their environments, citing security concerns.

I don't think DeepSeek will ever be a serious threat to OpenAI, Microsoft (MSFT), or Alphabet (GOOGL, GOOG) because it's unlikely a Western company will trust a Chinese company's technology with sensitive information. However, DeepSeek has opened up the path for open-source companies from Western nations, possibly introducing an app that could threaten the AI-as-a-Service business model.

What about Nvidia? If you believe the company's management team, Test Time Scaling will only increase demand for Nvidia's more powerful AI chips in the inference phase over more price-performant chips. Additionally, AI models are becoming increasingly more complex. Chief Executive Officer Jensen Huang said on the company's third-quarter earnings call:

On the other hand, the models are getting larger, they're multimodality. Just the number of dimensions that inference is innovating is incredible. And this innovation rate is what makes Nvidia's architecture so great because our ecosystem is fantastic. Everybody knows that if they innovate on top of CUDA on top of Nvidia's architecture, they can innovate more quickly and they know that everything should work. And if something were to happen, it's probably likely their code and not ours. And so that ability to innovate in every single direction at the same time, having a large installed base so that whatever you create could land on a Nvidia computer and be deployed broadly all around the world in every single data center all the way out to the edge into robotic systems, that capability is really quite phenomenal.

CEO Huang alludes to several advantages in the statement above that help keep it at the top of the AI chip market, including the CUDA platform, the ecosystem, developer tools, software, customer support, reliability, and a massive performance advantage over nearest competitors. A Seeking Alpha article quoting Evercore ISI analyst Mark Lipacis stated:

Nvidia is still the platform of choice for hyperscalers' customers, with it being 5 to 10 years ahead of "anything else in the market," Lipacis added. AMD (AMD) and Amazon Web Services are seen as a "distant" second and third, Lipacis explained. And while application specific integrated circuits [price-performant chips] will have a role, external workloads (such as cloud facing AWS, Google Cloud and Azure), and enterprise on-premise will likely remain dominated by Nvidia, Lipacis said.

The company also has several other defenses against a future where companies may shift away from more powerful chips toward more price-performant chips. For instance, Nvidia is a free cash flow (“FCF”) machine today, and it can invest heavily in developing price-performant chips if it sees that's the direction in which the market is evolving.

Nvidia may have started as a hardware company that manufactured GPUs. However, it is evolving into a company that provides end-to-end AI solutions. It gives customers software tools to build chatbots, AI virtual assistants, and virtual agents and now sells cloud services alongside the company's high-performance AI chips. So, it's more than just a chip provider—it's a full-fledged AI company.

It also emphasizes the Total Cost of Ownership (“TCO”) over its entire AI infrastructure solution rather than focusing solely on making the chip, which provides a powerful defense against competitors only selling price-performant chips. NVIDIA includes the whole hardware and software ecosystem, support, operating expenses, and the ability to deploy AI solutions rapidly into its TCO calculations. Management wants to be able to go to a customer and say, “Our AI chips may cost more upfront, but our whole AI solution saves money over the long term.”

So, although DeepSeek and momentum towards driving AI costs lower threaten Nvidia in the long term, it has ways of fighting back, and whether this risk will manifest adversely in the company's business remains highly uncertain.

Company fundamentals

Outstanding Hopper sales during the third quarter helped the Data Center segment revenue increase 17% sequentially and 112% year-on-year to $30.8 billion. Hopper is the name of a specific Nvidia GPU architecture family, and H200 is the name of its most advanced model within the Hopper family. Chief Financial Officer (“CFO”) Colette Kress said on the company's third quarter FY 2025 earnings call (emphasis added):

Sequentially, Nvidia H200 sales increased significantly to double-digit billions, the fastest product ramp in our company's history. The H200 delivers up to 2 times faster inference performance and up to 50% improved TCO. Cloud service providers [CSPs] were approximately half of our data center sales with revenue increasing more than 2 times year-on-year. CSPs deployed Nvidia H200 infrastructure and high-speed networking with installations scaling to tens of thousands of GPUs to grow their business and serve rapidly rising demand for AI training and inference workloads. Nvidia H200-powered cloud instances are now available from AWS, CoreWeave, and Microsoft Azure with Google Cloud and OCI [Oracle (ORCL) Cloud Infrastructure] coming soon. Alongside significant growth from our large CSPs, Nvidia GPU regional cloud revenue jumped 2 times year-on-year as North America, EMEA, and Asia Pacific regions ramped Nvidia cloud instances and sovereign cloud buildout.

Outside CSPs, it more than doubled its revenue from consumer internet companies buying its Hopper technology to power training next-generation AI models, multimodal and agentic AI, deep learning recommender engines, generative AI, and content creation. CFO Kress also said on the earnings call (emphasis added):

“Nvidia's Ampere [the architectural family before Hopper] and Hopper infrastructures are fueling inference revenue growth for customers. NVIDIA is the largest inference platform in the world.”

Management likely pointed out that Nvidia has the largest inference platform because that's where it is most vulnerable to losing market share in the long term to competitors with price-performant chips.

CFO Kress said on the earnings call that it shipped its first Blackwell samples to customers during the third quarter. Blackwell is the company's latest architectural family and is in high demand because it's the most powerful chip on the market today. Blackwell can dramatically reduce the training time of large AI models that once took weeks or even months on hardware from two or three years ago. In the rapidly evolving AI industry, the faster developers can bring their innovations to market, the better their chance of succeeding.

The CFO said on the earnings call:

Blackwell is now in the hands of all of our major partners and they are working to bring up their Data Centers. We are integrating Blackwell systems into the diverse Data Center configurations of our customers. Blackwell demand is staggering and we are racing to scale supply to meet the incredible demand customers are placing on us.

Investors can expect Blackwell to drive significant Data Center revenue growth over the next year.

Gaming revenue increased 14% sequentially and 15% year over year to $3.3 billion. Automotive revenue increased 30% sequentially and 72% year-over-year to $449 million. Professional Visualization revenue increased 7% sequentially and 17% year over year to $486 million.

Nvidia's third-quarter FY 2025 total revenue increased 17% sequentially and 94% year-over-year to $35.1 billion, above the company's guidance of $32.5 billion and beating analysts' estimates by $1.95 billion.

Nvidia's GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) expanded 60 basis points (“bps”) year over year to 74.6% due to a higher mix of higher-margin Data Center revenue. Non-GAAP gross margins remained flat year over year at 75%. However, GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins declined sequentially by 50 bps and 70 bps, respectively. A mix shift from mature H100 systems to the newer, more complex H200 systems was primarily responsible for lower sequential gross margins. When ramping up new hardware, gross margins typically decline until the company refines its manufacturing processes and supply chain.

The company's third quarter FY 2025 GAAP operating income increased 110% over the previous year's comparable quarter to $21.869 billion. GAAP operating margin expanded by 484 bps year over year to 62.33%. Its non-GAAP operating income increased 101% year over year to $23.276 billion. The non-GAAP operating margin was 66.34%, which was flat year over year.

Nvidia's GAAP and non-GAAP net income were $19.309 billion and $20.010 billion, respectively. It grew GAAP diluted earnings-per-share (“EPS”) by 109% to $0.78, beating analysts' estimates by $0.08. Non-GAAP diluted EPS grew by 103% year over year to $0.81, beating analysts' estimates by $0.06.

The company ended the third quarter with cash and marketable securities of $38.49 billion and long-term debt of $8.46 billion. Nvidia's trailing 12-month (“TTM”) cash from operations (“CFO”) was 52.05%, which means that for every $1 in sales, the company generates $0.52 in CFO—extremely impressive.

Nvidia's third-quarter FY 2025 CFO increased 22% sequentially and 140% year over year to $17.63 billion. TTM CFO was $58.96 billion.

The company's third quarter FY capital expenditures (“CapEx”) increased 192% year over year to $813 million, and TTM CapEx increased 82.25% year over year to $2.413 billion. Although Nvidia doesn't manufacture the chips it designs, it has high CapEx needs because it has evolved into an AI infrastructure company. CFO Colette Kress said on the earnings call,

“Our investments include building data centers for development of our hardware and software stacks and to support new introductions.”

As a result, Nvidia has more CapEx than a typical fabless semiconductor manufacturer.

Nvidia's third-quarter FY 2025 FCF grew 138% year over year to $16.814 billion. TTM FCF grew 223% year over year to $56.55 billion.

Management used some of its cash flow to repurchase $11.0 billion in shares and pay $245 million in cash dividends.

Management forecasts total revenue of $37.5 billion in the fourth quarter of FY 2025. If it meets this guidance, fourth-quarter revenue will rise 69.7% year over year, and full-year FY 2025 revenue will increase 111% over FY 2024 to $128.66 billion. Nvidia CFO Kress said the following about revenue guidance:

[Revenue guidance] incorporates continued demand for Hopper architecture and the initial ramp of our Blackwell products, while demand is greatly exceed supply, we are on track to exceed our previous Blackwell revenue estimate of several billion dollars as our visibility into supply continues to increase. On gaming, although sell-through was strong in Q3, we expect fourth-quarter revenue to decline sequentially due to supply constraints.

Management forecasts fourth quarter FY 2025 GAAP gross margins of 73.0% and non-GAAP gross margins of 73.5%. This guidance is well below the company's peak GAAP gross margins of 78.4% and non-GAAP gross margins of 78.9% in the first quarter of FY 2025. On the earnings call, CFO Kress stated the reason for the gross margin decline:

As Blackwell ramps, we expect gross margins to moderate to the low-70s. When fully ramped, we expect Blackwell margins to be in the mid-70s.

Later in the earnings call, analysts asked several questions about the Blackwell ramp and how long it would be before gross margins rebounded. Colette Kress responded by saying:

Could we reach the mid-70s in the second half of next year [Calendar year 2026]? And yes, I think it is reasonable assumption or a goal for us to do, but we'll just have to see how that mix of ramp goes. But yes, it is definitely possible.

Analysts are highly interested in when gross margins will rebound because this can impact FCF and EPS, the numbers people use to value the stock. A prolonged decline in gross margins could adversely affect investors' valuation of the stock.

When the company reports its fourth-quarter results on February 26, note what management reports for the first quarter FY 2026 guidance. Currently, consensus analyst revenue estimates are for $41.94 billion. If the company's guidance exceeds those revenue estimates, the market will likely take it as a sign that the DeepSeek news was a non-event. If management's first-quarter guidance comes under analysts' estimates, concerns about DeepSeek could resurface.

Valuation

Nvidia's price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 28.92. With the significant decline in the company's market cap over the last several months, it trades closer to its three-year median, and some may consider it fairly valued. Yet others may believe the market still overvalues the stock based on where it trades above its five-, seven-, and ten-year median.

The company's price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is 52.44, which is well above the information technology (“IT”) sector's median P/E of 32.63, which may lead some to consider it overvalued. However, some may think the market undervalues it, based on the stock's P/E trading at 38.18% below its five-year average P/E.

Nvidia's one-year forward P/E-to-growth is 0.59 (One-year forward P/E of 29.90 divided by analysts' estimated EPS growth of 50.43%). Typically, growth investors will allow a stock to reach a PEG ratio of 2.0 before considering it overvalued. According to that convention, the market may vastly undervalue Nvidia's EPS growth over the next year. If its PEG ratio were 1.0, the stock price would be $223.91, a 71% rise above its February 12 closing stock price of $131.14. If its PEG ratio were 2.0, the stock price would be $447.81.

Let's analyze Nvidia using a reverse discount cash flow (“DCF”). This DCF uses a terminal growth rate of 4% because the company should continue steadily growing its cash flow well above the market average after the ten-year analysis period. I use a discount rate of 9%, which is the opportunity cost of investing in Nvidia, reflecting a less-than-moderate risk level. This reverse DCF uses an unlevered FCF for the following analysis.

Reverse DCF

The third quarter of FY 2025 reported Free Cash Flow TTM

 $56,550
Terminal growth rate 4%
Discount Rate 9%
Years 1-10 growth rate 17.9%
Stock Price (February 12, 2024, closing price) $131.14
Terminal FCF value $301.180 billion
Discounted Terminal Value $2313.120 billion
FCF margin 49.92%

If Nvidia can maintain a 50% FCF margin for the next ten years, the company must grow revenue by 17.9% annually to justify the February 12, 2025, closing price. Is this feasible? Its revenue has grown by around 31% over the last ten years, and in a previous article on the company, I explained why I believe the company can achieve at least 20% annual revenue growth over the next ten years. Analysts estimate Nvidia's revenue will grow at a 22.25% CAGR over the next ten years. Hence, a 17.9% yearly growth rate is within the realm of possibility.

However, a nearly 50% FCF margin is likely Nvidia's peak. In a market with rising competition, it is unlikely to sustain a 50% FCF margin over the next ten years. Broadcom (AVGO), a peer, achieved a peak FCF margin of 49.23% over the last ten years but has a median FCF margin of 40.28% in the previous ten years. Let's assume NVIDIA's FCF averages 40% over the next ten years; at an 18% annual growth rate, the estimated intrinsic value would be $106.19. Assuming the company grows revenue at analysts' estimated growth rate of around 22% annually over the next ten years, the estimated intrinsic value would be $143.62. A revenue growth rate of 20.8% over the next ten years at an average FCF margin of 40% would justify the February 12, 2025, stock price of $131.14.

The above reverse DCF uses many assumptions, and no one should believe that the company will hit any of the numbers above exactly. I used the above reverse DCF to loosely determine what the current stock price implies about the market's expectations for the company's FCF margin and revenue growth rates.

Nvidia's price-to-FCF is 57.63, well below its three-, five-, and seven-year median. It only exceeds its ten-year median. Some may consider the stock undervalued because of where its price-to-FCF trades compared to its medians. However, Seeking Alpha's quant grades the stock's valuation as a D-.

Risks

Nvidia had at least three customers, who accounted for more than 10% of revenue in the three- and nine-month period ending on October 27. Additionally, it has an indirect customer who purchased through customer C and resellers, which makes forecasting demand from that customer much more difficult. Nvidia's reliance on a small number of customers means the company has a customer concentration risk. Nvidia could underperform investors' revenue and profitability expectations if any of its top customers have financial issues or cancel or reduce orders.

Additionally, the AI market is rapidly evolving. If Nvidia misses a new industry trend or fails to adapt quickly to industry changes, it could lose market share in a competitive market. Although I don't think DeepSeek's technology presents a real threat to Nvidia's business, it made people aware that if a company or technology does appear that can legitimately reduce the need for increasingly more powerful chips to train and run AI models, it could potentially threaten Nvidia's position as top AI chip provider by making price-performant chips more desirable.

Nvidia stock remains a buy

As I said in a previous article, most buy-and-hold investors would be better off waiting for the stock's FCF yield to rise above 2% before buying. Despite the stock's high returns over the last several years, I have been leery about recommending it for long-term investors since it may not have been a good value to invest in long-term since 2022. At an FCF yield of 1.73%, risk-averse investors should avoid it, considering a potential selloff could be steep if the company misses revenue, EPS, and FCF expectations.

However, this company has a high potential upside for at least the next year or two. Analysts expect revenue to grow 51.69% year over year in FY 2026, which is impressive for a company with $113.27 billion in TTM revenue. That revenue growth comes on the back of Blackwell's ramping over the next year and recent earnings reports from cloud companies indicating they are increasing CapEx in 2025, with much of that spending likely winding up with NVIDIA; aggressive growth investors interested in the continuing adoption of AI and willing to accept the risk of the stock's potential downside should consider buying. I rate Nvidia a buy for aggressive growth investors only.

data.title: Slate Grocery REIT: My Favorite High Monthly Dividend Paying REIT
data.description:
My previous piece on Slate Grocery REIT (OTC:SRRTF) was issued back in August, 2024, when my decision was to put the REIT on my watchlist because of the near-term uncertainties around the maturing bond refinancings. However, when Slate reported its Q3 2024 earnings, it already became clear that management has and will manage the debt rollovers very successfully, introducing no meaningful headwinds to the AFFO and dividend coverage. So, since the other fundamentals were, in my opinion, robust and much more valuable than what was priced in by the market, I decided to open a significant position in Slate. Currently, it is my second largest REIT right after Realty Income (NYSE:O).

Recently, Slate issued its Q4 2024 earnings report, which just confirms how compelling the overall thesis is here.

Slate trades at a P/FFO of ~9x and offers a dividend yield of 8.9%. At the same time, the fundamentals remain robust and the organic growth continues to come in strong.

Let me explain in more detail, why I am planning to add even more to my existing position in Slate.

Thesis update

All in all, Q4 was business as usual for Slate. The same store NOI increased by 4.3% on a TTM basis, which was almost purely driven by the higher rents. And the key reason for higher rents has been clearly the positive results on the leasing spread front. As we can see in the chart below, Slate has consistently managed to resign leases at materially higher rates than before.

If we look at the lease expirations, then we will notice a smooth picture, where there is not a single year on the horizon (until 2028) that would require Slate to renegotiate a significant chunk of the existing leases. On the one hand it might limit the growth potential or the pace at which Slate comes closer to the market-level rents, but, on the other hand, it decreases the risks of temporary vacancies and elevated CapEx spend.

Speaking of Slate's near to medium-term growth prospects, I think we have to assume a rather similar trajectory as the previous two or three years. Namely, since the total amount of leases that come due each year is balanced, the convergence between Slate in-place rents and market-level rents should provide strong enough impact to drive the NOI growth at around 5% on a quarter-to-quarter basis.

Here I would like to really emphasize the main advantage of Slate: the average in-place rent of $12.65 PSF is well below the market average of $23.80. This offers a significant tailwind for management to keep registering attractive same-store NOI growth rates, which is important in the financial market environment like we have now, where M&A activity remains somewhat muted.

However, if we look at the comment on the Q4 earnings call from Blair Welch, Chief Executive Officer of Slate, we should not rule out some minor M&A moves in 2025:

So, you know we’re creative at Slate Asset Management. We recognize that we have a discount from where we trade. So we talk to the Board all the time about capital allocation. We are currently underwriting literally billions of grocery deals and retail deals in the U.S., but we’ll be thoughtful and disciplined on how we do that. Do I think there is going to be some deals to do this year? Yes, but we’re sensitive and making sure we do the right deals for the unitholders that create long-term value. We think about that all the time and we’ll be careful. But we want to buy good real estate and then we think we can.

Having said that, my key takeaway from this message is that the CEO has the confidence to venture into M&A, which implies that the balance sheet is healthy. In my previous article, I had some concerns about Slate's balance sheet or rather the effects on the FFO/AFFO generation after the 2024 debt refinancing.

In this respect, it is worth mentioning the following two transactions:

  • December 2024, Slate sourced a $33.0 million mortgage at an interest rate of 5.51% and a maturity year in 2030.
  • November 2024, Slate sourced a $100.5 million mortgage at an interest rate of 6.40% and a maturity year in 2029.

These two transactions were made relatively close to the previous interest rates, and have now significantly decreased the refinancing risk.

So, as of Q4 2024, the FFO and AFFO payout ratios stood at 86% and 109%, respectively. I know that AFFO payout of 109% does not sound sustainable, but here is the thing:

  • Cash tax expense was abnormally high this quarter (~$0.6m above the same quarter last year).
  • Financing charges were also almost two times above the usual amount due to an intensive debt financing activity (~$0.5m above the same quarter last year).
  • The leasing costs and tenant-related CapEx were a bit elevated due to material releasing volumes.

If we normalized these items, the dividend would be easily covered by the AFFO generation, which would then match the CEO's sentiment about potential M&A moves.

The bottom line

Slate's investment case is straightforward and backed by defensive cash flows. The significant gap between the in-place rents and the market-average rents in combination with balanced lease maturities will very likely drive the like-for-like NOI growth further, which should clearly strengthen the underlying AFFO generation.

Given the above and the fact that the refinancing risk is well-mitigated, P/FFO of ~9x seems just too cheap.

In my view, Slate is a solid long-term investment pick, which can reward investors through stable and juicy dividends, while waiting for the thesis to play out.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Not All 2% Spreads Are Created Equal
data.description:
Real estate works on spreads – the cap rate at which a property is acquired versus the cost of financing that property. It is the REIT version of ROIC – WACC.

A positive spread is required for investment to be viable, and the spread must be sufficiently large to make it worth taking the risk. As such, cap rates tend to fluctuate with the cost of capital. There is a lag time of unpredictable duration, but over long stretches of time, cap rates will roughly move in parallel with the cost of capital.

The past 3 years have been an excellent example of this process at work. At the start of 2022 when interest rates were near 0, cap rates were very low by historical standards. Due to the aforementioned lag times, spreads shrank and widened throughout the 3-year period, but over the long run, the relationship held true.

Today, cap rates are substantially higher along with both equity and debt cost of capital being higher.

So in early 2022, the situation was roughly 6% acquisition cap rates and 4% cost of capital. Today it is closer to 8% acquisition cap rates and 6% cost of capital.

Parallel Shift

These 8, 6, and 4 numbers we are throwing out are rough ballpark figures. Cap rates vary significantly by sector, property age, and growth rates, while the cost of capital varies by company-specific valuation and credit rating. That said, the directionality and rough magnitude of the move are demonstrable.

The 10-year treasury spent early 2022 in the mid 2% range and now sits in the mid 4% range.

Interest rates on senior notes and secured debt roughly followed this 200 basis point shift.

Similarly, in early 2022 REITs traded around 22X FFO, while today they trade at a median of 13.0X. That takes the cost of equity capital from 4.54% in early 2022 to 7.7% today – a bit more than 200 basis points.

Cap rates are adjusted to keep spreads similar, so the spread remains at about 2%. At first glance, the same spread appears to have the same economics.

  • $100 of property bought at an 8% cap rate with a 6% cost of capital is net accretive by $2.
  • $100 of property bought at a 6% cap rate with a 4% cost of capital is net accretive by $2.

It seems to be the same, and I think the market is viewing it as equivalent.

Not All 2% Spreads Are Equal

I posit that the 8% cap rate and 6% cost of capital are substantially better than the 6 and 4 of early 2022.

Specifically, there are 5 ways in which the higher cap rate and higher cost of capital result in better long-term total returns despite the spread being the same.

  1. More accretive organic growth
  2. More accretive reinvestment
  3. More accretive debt paydown
  4. More accretive buybacks
  5. Bidirectional valuation adjustment

Before we get into each of these advantages, I think it is helpful to clarify that property-level performance is an entirely separate thing from the financial considerations that go into the purchase.

A given property will produce the same NOI and NOI growth whether it is purchased at an 8 cap with a 6% cost of capital or a 6 cap with a 4% cost of capital. The property is the same, so the leasing and revenues will be the same. Property-level expenses will also be the same. It is just a lower purchase price in relation to in-place NOI that results in the 8 cap instead of the 6 cap.

More Accretive Organic Growth

The same amount of growth has a greater impact in the 8 and 6 environments, than it did in the 6 and 4 environment.

Why?

Well, the cost of capital is largely locked in at purchase. Equity is permanent capital, and most real estate is financed with long-term fixed rate debt. So in 5 years after purchase, the cost of capital on that purchase will still be the initial 6% of the purchase price.

The NOI, however, changes and usually grows. Let’s assume a fairly normal growth rate of 3% and apply it to an 8% cap rate acquisition with a 6% cost of capital and a 6% cap rate acquisition with a 4% cost of capital.

Even though the going-in spread on each was 2%, the larger NOI in the 8 and 6 environment makes growth more effective, such that in year 5 the cash flow accretion from a $100 acquisition is $3.27 compared to $2.96 in the 6 and 4 environments.

Both had 2% spreads. Both had 3% growth rates. The growth just has more impact in the 8 and 6 environment.

More accretive reinvestment, dividends, debt reduction, and buybacks

With positive spreads, REITs have some cash flow to play with. That capital is largely allocated into 4 buckets:

  1. Reinvesting in more properties
  2. Returning capital to shareholders via dividends
  3. Returning capital to shareholders via buybacks
  4. Paying down debt

All 4 capital allocations are more accretive in the 8 and 6 environment than the 6 and 4.

Properties purchased with retained cash flow do not have a cost of capital, so the spread on cash flow purchased properties is 8% now, as opposed to 6% before.

Capital returned to shareholders goes further as the lower FFO multiples make buybacks more accretive. Buying back stock trading at 13X FFO is functionally a 7.7% ROIC while buying back stock in early 2022 would have been less than 5% ROIC.

Dividends paid to shareholders compound better today because dividend yields are substantially higher.

Reinvesting dividends at 4% grows income much faster than reinvesting at below 3%.

Every dollar spent on reducing debt increases AFFO by more if it is paying off higher-cost debt. Thus, debt paydown is more accretive in the 8 and 6 environment than in the 6 and 4.

Growth At A Sector Level

REIT growth has been toward the weaker side for the past few years. For the reasons discussed above, I believe that was largely a result of the zero-interest rate environment. While spreads were still there, making it look like acquisitions could be accretive, 6 and 4 is a terrible acquisition environment.

Today’s environment is much healthier. 8% cap rates make for stronger external and organic growth. The market seems to think higher interest rates are bad for REITs.

I strongly disagree. Moderate to slightly high rates are very healthy for REITs.

The pain came in the adjustment from zero to high. This brings us to our last discussion point as to why 8 and 6 is much better than 6 and 4.

Bidirectional Valuation Adjustment

When treasuries were at rock bottom, there was only one way they could go – up. All real estate is valued on cap rates, and interest rates are a major component of the denominator.

Thus, the denominator was as small as it could reasonably be, meaning that property values could only be adjusted down.

With today’s more normal interest rates, valuation adjustment could be in either direction. The denominator could go a little higher or a little lower. Scenario-weighted adjustments to the denominator largely offset, such that the value change of real estate assets is instead driven by the numerator – NOI.

Overall, real estate is in a much better place today than it was in early 2022 despite acquisition spreads being nominally the same.

data.title: Blackstone: Start Buying The Dip (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Our recent call to downgrade Blackstone (NYSE:BX) from "Strong Buy" to "Sell" has been vindicated by the stock's recent underperformance. Since our previous article was published on 23 November 2024, Blackstone's share price has fallen by around -19.7% (-19.1% including dividends).

This sharp double-digit decline in less than 3 months would have been a significant setback for investors, especially given that the S&P 500 Index (SPX) gained 2.4% over the same period.

To be clear, we have been bullish on Blackstone's long-term prospects since we initiated coverage back in January 2023. At the time, unwarranted fears of a potential real estate crisis and concerns over rising redemptions at Blackstone's flagship BREIT fund provided a rare opportunity to invest at a Forward P/E of just 14.8x. That kind of valuation was extremely attractive for a high-quality alternative asset manager such as Blackstone.

By 23 November 2024, Blackstone had not only fully recovered from the bear market and made new highs, but its valuations had also become ridiculously expensive, at a Forward P/E of 44.0x. We saw this as an opportunity to take profit on the stock and recycle capital by tactically rotating into less risky assets for protection or into other stocks that provided more value and upside potential.

Not Expensive, But Not Cheap Either

Following the recent selloff, Blackstone is currently trading at a more reasonable Forward P/E of 28.0x. As the above chart shows, much of the speculative sentiment driving up the stock price in November has dissipated. Blackstone's valuations are now more realistic and in line with the company's long-term growth prospects.

Although current valuations are by no means cheap, we believe that accelerated dealmaking and capital deployment at Blackstone into areas such as data centers and renewable energy infrastructure will provide a material boost to assets under management (AUM) growth and fee-related earnings in the next 2 to 3 years.

Instead of waiting and hoping for valuations to get cheaper, which we think is unlikely unless there are new adverse shocks to the company, we prefer to be more decisive and start accumulating the stock at current levels. Accordingly, we are upgrading our rating on Blackstone from a "Sell" to a "Buy" for now. Should the stock decline further, we would accumulate more aggressively.

Q4 2024 Results Broadly Positive, But Real Estate Drags

Blackstone's recent underperformance is partly due to its concentrated exposure to the real estate sector. Activity in this sector remains subdued due to elevated interest rates, which restrict economic growth while imposing a higher discount rate on real estate valuations. However, we continue to see real estate as an attractive sector that should outperform as the Federal Reserve (Fed) resumes its rate-cut cycle.

Apart from real estate, the other core segments of Blackstone's portfolio are witnessing strong returns.

Admittedly, we were surprised by the higher-than-expected print for January's CPI data, which may keep the Fed's rate-cut cycle on hold till the second half of the year. Nonetheless, we expect inflation to continue cooling in the coming months, and we see the occasional bump in inflation data as nothing more than a temporary setback rather than a serious threat to the outlook on inflation.

Even with real estate dragging down the portfolio, Blackstone's overall business has performed extremely well, with fee-related earnings growth of 21% and AUM growth of 8% for FY2024.

Investors appear to be underappreciating the real estate sector's potential at the moment. We suspect this is due to impatient investors who would prefer to chase the hottest themes of the day rather than invest in fundamentally sound sectors with the potential to generate decent alpha over the next 2 to 3 years.

We remain bullish on real estate and see Blackstone as a quality pick to express our bullish view and to build portfolio exposure to the sector.

Growing Demand For Data Centers

In recent years, data centers have become a core focus of Blackstone, and we like that management is aggressively pursuing deals in this segment to fully capitalize on long-term demand.

According to data published by International Data Corporations (IDC), artificial intelligence (AI) is expected to accelerate growth in data collection, processing, and storage in the coming years.

According to comments from Nadeem Meghji, the Global Co-Head of Blackstone Real Estate, growing demand and tighter supply constraints have driven up data center rents by 84%. One of Blackstone's largest data center assets, QTS, has grown by more than 900% in size over just three and a half years.

We believe that Blackstone's recent acquisition of AirTrunk for A$24 billion has the potential to unlock even greater value for investors in the coming years. Asia Pacific demand for data centers is still lagging behind the U.S., but we are witnessing growing momentum in the region in trying to play catch up on the development of data centers as well as related energy infrastructure.

Blackstone is clearly doubling down on demand for data centers, and we view the stock as an attractive proxy for investing in the AI boom without having to pay a ridiculous price multiple (see Magnificent Seven).

Potential Versus Price

One question many readers may have is whether Blackstone's business prospects have already been fully reflected in its current share price. One crude way to gauge this is to observe current market sentiment on AI themes and see where most investors and fund managers are putting their money to work to try and ride the AI boom.

Based on what we are reading in the financial media and across-the-board analyst research, it is clear that the hottest trades on AI remain heavily skewed towards the big tech hyperscalers, AI chipmakers, and generative AI companies. Needless to say, valuations of companies linked to these themes are ridiculously expensive due to overcrowded trades.

Such observations are critical to our stock selection process. We like that Blackstone has positioned itself to be a major beneficiary of the AI boom but that the market has been distracted by the underperformance of its real estate segment, which, we think, is undervalued. With Blackstone being excluded from the hottest AI themes out there, investors can position themselves for the AI boom while not having to pay ridiculous prices for that exposure.

Risk To Outlook

The biggest risk to our bullish outlook on Blackstone is the ever-evolving and disruptive nature of innovative technologies across AI-related themes. Just as the recent release of DeepSeek surprised everyone and challenged long-held assumptions about how AI will evolve and develop over the coming years, Blackstone's aggressive push into data centers comes with risks.

Fortunately, such risks can be managed to a certain extent by investing in a well-diversified portfolio. Just like how we have always been reminding our readers to stick to good investing basics of diversification and to invest in value, we remind our readers not to get too excited over Blackstone's data center exposure. Blackstone should be viewed as a high-quality and high-performing alternative asset manager that one is willing to keep as a long-term investment.

In Conclusion

We see an opportunity to take advantage of the recent pullback in Blackstone's stock price by buying the dip, considering our bullish outlook on the company's prospects over the next 2 to 3 years.

However, given that Blackstone is not exactly cheap at a Forward P/E of 28.0x, we see the case for a more cautious rating on the stock. Accordingly, we are upgrading our rating on Blackstone from a "Sell" to a "Buy" for now. Should the stock decline further, we would consider accumulating more aggressively.

data.title: Not DIY Investor? Here's Where I Would Invest $80,000 If I Was A Beginner
data.description:
Introduction

I recently helped out a friend who wanted to start his investing journey. With his Navy retirement rapidly approaching (he still has some years), he reached out for some advice on where to invest $80,000. For transparency, I am not a financial advisor but enjoy helping my closest family & friends when it comes to investing.

Investing on your own can be intimidating, and I completely understand why investors hire financial advisors or prefer to place their money into something simple like a high-yield savings account or Certificate of Deposit (CD). For me, I prefer to invest on my own. That way, if something goes wrong, I have no one to blame but myself.

To be honest, this article applies to anyone with any lump sum of money. In this article, I discuss where I would invest $80,000 right now.

Life After Retirement

As many may know I served in the U.S. Navy for 21 years, retiring in late 2023. For me retirement wasn't as scary as it is for some. But depending on your lifestyle and financial obligations, it can be.

For me, I've always lived below my means. But many of my friends like to buy expensive cars and other items, as the military is one of the most reliable streams of income.

Every 1st and 15th you're going to get paid. And your chances of your income being suddenly cut is highly unlikely.

But what many seem to forget is your career will come to an end one day, whether you serve 20, 30, or even 40 years. And if you're not financially strategic, you're likely going to need to find a job once your career is over.

To be honest, my friend is not a do-it-yourself investor and likely never will be. He just doesn't have the temperament for it. So, when he reached out to me to help him, I wasn't surprised.

As a beginner, I knew I had to be strategic about where to tell him to invest. So, without further ado, here is a good place to invest your money if you're a beginner with a longer time horizon before retirement.

Investment #1

Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) is where I would invest a sizable portion of my capital. Especially, since they announced several splits last year.

At the time of writing, SCHG trades at less than $29 a share and gives a beginner's portfolio nice exposure to the technology sector. They also track the performance of the Dow Jones (DJI).

Currently, technology makes up roughly 47% of SCHG's holdings. They also have double-digit exposure to the Consumer Cyclical, Communication (XLC), and Health Care sectors (XLV). This provides some protection should the economy experience sudden downturns like a recession.

Some of SCHG's most popular holdings are the infamous Magnificent 7 including Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), NVIDIA Corp (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), and Tesla (TSLA). Investing in SCHG makes sense since the average forward price-to-earnings of these companies is over 50x, utterly expensive.

As a whole the S&P is expensive with a P/E ratio of nearly 29x as the index saw 24% returns over the past two years.

Moreover, SCHG is perfect for those non do-it-yourself investors as they have great technology exposure and very low fees. Their expense ratio of 0.04% is low in comparison to peers like Vanguard Mega Cap Growth Index Fund ETF Shares (MGK) & Invesco Large Cap Growth ETF (PWB) whose ratios were 0.07% and 0.56% respectively.

This means if you invest $10,000 into SCHG you would only pay $4 to the manager of the fund annually. And if you're someone who invests with a financial advisor or buy into funds with expensive fees, this could be a huge risk as this could eat into your returns. So, finding with strong returns and low fees are essential.

Looking at SCHG's returns, the fund has handily beaten the S&P over the past 1, 3, 5, and 10-year periods. And although past performance is no indicator of future performance, I suspect the fund will continue to deliver returns above the S&P's historical average of 7% - 10% going forward as tech will likely continue to see higher growth for the foreseeable future.

Investment #2

The next investment for a beginner is the SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG). While SCHG tracks the performance of the Dow Jones Index, SPLG tracks the performance of the S&P, giving you exposure to both indexes and mitigating underperformance risks.

SPLG is a fund that is overlooked by investors in my opinion, likely due to the popularity of sister funds like SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 High Dividend ETF (SPYD), and SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG).

But SPLG actually has the lowest expense ratio of all four funds at just 0.02%. That's even lower than SCHG's 0.04%. SPY had the highest at 0.09% while SPYD & SPYG had ratios of 0.07% and 0.04% respectively. SPLG also had the lowest share price of the group with the exception of SPYD at roughly $70 a share.

SPLG also has a decent amount of exposure to technology but gives you higher exposure to the financial sector at 13.63%. SCHG only has a little over 7% exposure.

So, this means you get more exposure to too big to fail banks like JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Bank of America (BAC). You also get exposure to financial powerhouses like Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA) as well.

Moreover, investing in SPLG also gives you a higher dividend yield, not a surprise since they have more exposure to the financial sector (XLF). SPLG's forward yield is currently 1.23% while SCHG's is 0.38%. So, investing into these two funds give you a little bit of yield to go along with expected growth.

Below are the returns for the fund over the past decade. As stated previously, the S&P has returned over 24% the past two years, but this could be drastically different going forward. And this could lead to underperformance, a risk of investing in funds.

Risks Of Underperformance

According to Goldman Sachs (GS), the S&P is expected to grow only 10% by the end of this year, less than half it did the past two years. Moreover, EPS is expected to grow 11% and significantly slow to 7% in 2026.

If tariffs are implemented, this could cause inflation to resurge, leading to even lower returns than predicted if the FED keeps rates higher for longer or are forced to raise rates.

More specifically, SCHG could underperform due to further sell-offs in tech as a result of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), which caused a brief disruption earlier this month.

Allocation Suggestion

Now for the good stuff, how I would allocate $80,000 right now. My friend was comfortable allocating $35k to each fund. He wanted to invest the entire amount, but I suggested keeping $10,000 in case he wanted to research and find individual stocks to invest in on his own.

I also advised him that keeping some cash in case of a market correction or pullback was important. This is so he could continue to dollar cost average into each holding.

As a scenario, I showed him what his portfolio would be worth if he had invested the same amount into each fund when he first joined the Navy 15 years ago. His $80,000 portfolio would be worth more than $600,000 currently if he reinvested his dividends.

Some individual stocks worth looking into right now if you were considering taking a stab at individual stocks are PepsiCo (PEP), Verizon (VZ), VICI Properties (VICI), or Realty Income (O). Especially, considering the market is expected to see lower returns the next few years.

All four companies are well-established businesses that are likely to continue paying dividends for the foreseeable future. They also currently have attractive valuations and add nice starting yields to a portfolio mainly focused on growth.

Conclusion

If you're not a do-it-yourself investor and hesitant to invest money into individual stocks, both SCHG & SPLG are great options currently. Especially, considering the average P/E of some of the most popular technology stocks.

Moreover, as a whole, the S&P index is expensive with a P/E of nearly 29x due to the index's strong returns the past two years.

And while the S&P is still projected to see 10% returns this year but could slow thereafter. And adding higher yielding stocks to your portfolio could be a great choice, especially those currently trading at attractive valuations.

data.title: DocuSign: AI Agents Are A Game Changer
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

DocuSign (NASDAQ:DOCU) shares are up a little over 10% since the last time I wrote on them back in early December. I believe a big driver in this increase in shares for the e-signature giant has been the company’s Q3 earnings report along with stronger AI models making their transition to a document management company more evident. While Q3 data is a little old, the trend is clear: Earnings data shows the company is using AI to help customers with contract management and this is improving sales.

These AI features are helping other companies, DocuSign’s customers, cut costs on contract management and compliance. This continues to be a multi-trillion dollar opportunity for the company. It’s the biggest reason why I am so bullish on the company. AI agents for contracts will be huge.

With this, I continue to believe shares are a strong buy.

Why I’m Doing Follow-Up Coverage

Even with how much DocuSign shares have beaten the market over the last few months, I still think there is a lot more room for the company to grow. I think the 7.36% run up since their last earnings report is just the start.

When I last wrote on DocuSign (pre-Q3), I saw the company laying the groundwork for their IAM system to be a huge value add for customers. Two of the big reasons were DocuSign’s partnership with Legitify and also the newly launched platform called “DocuSign Transactions.”

Then, I explained how I believed the partnership with Legitify would help DocuSign expand its reach into Europe, which obviously would benefit the company since this is a high dollar mature market that's heavy on AI and data compliance. With this, DocuSign’s platform, “DocuSign Transactions” would allow for documents to more easily be signed by clients and agents who are in the real estate industry. Real estate transactions are a multi trillion dollar, still mostly paper-based industry. DocuSign is ripe to disrupt it.

I remain incredibly optimistic. In 2025, with even newer AI models like OpenAI’s o3 now commercially available, DocuSign’s key AI use cases are expanding. This is why I am doing follow-up coverage.

New AI models are making it even more possible. The story was exciting before. I think it's even more exciting now

AI Agents Are A Game Changer

While earnings call data is older, I think the story is more pressing and relevant than ever. During their earnings call from December 5th, DocuSign CEO Allan Thygesen explained how the company is expanding its reach all around the world through the power of its new AI models and the expansion of IAM (Intelligent Agreement Management).

We launched an upgraded search experience that includes predictive typeahead functionality, more filters, and the ability to export results, Thygesen said.

We also expanded the availability of IAM to more geographies. In early October, IAM with DocuSign Maestro, our automated agreement workflow builder shipped to all regions where DocuSign operates, including North America, Latin America, EMEA, and most countries in APAC.

Also, just this week, we released new AI features in Navigator across five major markets, Australia, Canada, France, Germany, and the UK. In these countries, we've created AI models that meet local regulatory and compliance requirements. In November, we began making IAM available for department-level use cases for enterprise customers.

What’s most exciting for DocuSign and its investors is that the TAM for AI Agents is expected to grow almost exponentially over the next five years. According to PR Newswire, the anticipation is that the AI Agents market will grow from $5.1 billion in 2024 to a staggering $47.1 billion in 2030. When we add this $47.1 billion to the TAM of legal agents of $2 trillion (from my research last year), this gives us a total of about $2.05 trillion of market opportunity. I think DocuSign is in a great position to grab a good chunk of this ~$2.05 trillion in market value. Their new IAM product has the chance to really lead in both markets.

The thought process behind the AI Agent growth was explained:

This growth trajectory reflects a robust compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 44.8% over the forecast period, the article read.

AI agent adoption's crucial determinant is the increasing demand for automation that enhances efficiency, scale and decision-making in diverse sectors. As businesses are constantly pressed to streamline operations while minimizing operational expenses, AI agents offer an effective alternative through automating repetitive functions, analyzing big datasets and providing real-time actionable insights.

In my opinion, DocuSign’s AI and unique contract management system have an immense potential to capture some of this incredible growth over the next five years. This is what has me optimistic about the company.

On January 3rd, DocuSign announced that it will be using even more GenAI for contracts and agreements. The company released a blog post:

[we have developed] new capabilities rooted in AI, like document highlighting or remote ID verification, to help transform the way our customers work.

According to Relevance AI, specific use cases for AI Agents in DocuSign’s business include uses in places like real estate that I mentioned before. But now, healthcare is becoming a key place where IAM technology can add a ton of value. The article from Relevance AI argues that AI Agents will help DocuSign redefine patient care and compliance in the healthcare industry.

Valuation

I want to dive into valuation by first looking at growth. DocuSign’s revenue and EPS both currently clock in at a premium to the sector median. Really, top line growth here is one of the most important growth metrics for any tech investment.

Looking first at the forward revenue growth, DocuSign’s forward revenue is estimated to come in at a 7.78% YoY forward growth rate. When compared to the sector median of 6.10%, this is a 27.51% premium to the sector median. Seeking Alpha Quant gives DocuSign a grade of a C+ on this metric. I disagree. This is 27.5% better growth. This is excellent growth.

Looking at their forward EPS diluted growth, the story is even better. DocuSign’s forward projected EPS growth of 22.33% YoY is a 141.40% premium to the sector median of 9.25%. This is given a grade of a B+ by Seeking Alpha Quant. To me, this is an A+. This forward EPS growth well outstrips the sector median.

So while DocuSign comes in strong on EPS and revenue growth, the company’s forward GAAP price-to-earnings ratio is well below the sector median. Seeking Alpha gives it a grade of an A-. DocuSign’s forward GAAP P/E of 18.14 is 38.84% lower than the sector median of 29.65.

With this, I think expected growth for DocuSign should pick up as AI expands the use cases of their IAM system to go after this $2 trillion TAM market.

Because of this, I think shares should converge on the sector median forward GAAP P/E of 29.65. If we saw shares converge at this rate, up from 18.14, this would represent 63.45% upside.

Risks

While stronger AI models bring an incredible opportunity, I think the biggest risk is that competition is rising due to new competitors building on these same AI models. New AI agents for legal documents are serious competitors to the brand and reputation that DocuSign has built.

In one example, Harvey AI is an AI agent that is designed specifically for lawyers and legal professionals to help audit contracts and speed up legal due diligence. This somewhat encroaches on the IAM market opportunity. Clio.com dives into Harvey AI:

Harvey AI is a legal AI platform designed for legal professionals. This tool can help lawyers draft, analyze, and answer questions, conduct legal research, identify issues, and much more.

Through the same OpenAI models:

[Harvey AI] assists with contract analysis, due diligence, litigation, and regulatory compliance and can help generate insights, recommendations, and predictions based on data. As a result, lawyers can deliver faster and more cost-effective solutions for client issues.

Harvey AI differentiates from ChatGPT in the sense that it is able to support — and specializes in — legal work. Harvey AI has already partnered with some of the largest law firms in the world. According to On The Map Internet Marketing, 3,500 lawyers from Allen & Overy, a London-based law firm, tested the AI tool by asking it 40,000 questions.

Although this all sounds very promising for Harvey AI, I think many, if not all of these risks can be mitigated because DocuSign controls the key contract data. Controlling the core signed contracts means you should become the base default platform. So while some of Harvey’s key AI features don’t encroach on DocuSign, DocuSign knows they are the core vendor here. Contracts have to come from DocuSign to Harvey for analysis. Not vice versa. In the long run, this should give DocuSign room for their IAM offering to be integral to using a tool like Harvey AI.

Bottom Line

While I would like revenue growth to accelerate, I think DocuSign is in a fantastic position. The company has expanded its reach and seems to be making the most of the future of even more powerful AI models.

AI Agents in both the legal industry and in the contract field will play a big role in the development of contract management platforms. DocuSign is already one step ahead.

With this, I continue to believe shares are still a strong buy.
